segments.text: Welcome to your daily dose of robotics news for June 26, 2026!
Today we cover Sony AI’s amazing ROS feat, key business lessons from automation failures, and the future of self-driving research.
Nine stories await you right now.
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: Sony AI's Ace (Nature cover, beat a pro table tennis player) — running on ROS 2."
The report details the success of Sony AI’s robot named Ace in defeating professional human players in competitive table tennis matches.

This performance demonstrates advanced robotic capabilities, specifically noting the system's ability to track fast-spinning balls moving up to twenty meters per second and perceive them within mere milliseconds.
Operationally, the robot maintained impressive real-time control, executing complex decisions on a one kilohertz loop rate.
The core significance lies in its successful integration and operation utilizing the ROS 2 framework for physical AI applications in high-speed competitive settings.
This achievement signals considerable advancement potential for deploying sophisticated artificial intelligence within demanding, dynamic sporting environments.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Great Robots, Failed Companies: The Business Foundations Automation Startups Skip,"
reporting that achieving operational success in automation companies requires more than just technical innovation.

The analysis highlights that while building functional robotic systems meets a basic entry threshold for these businesses, the critical weakness often appears in establishing necessary surrounding business infrastructure.

Numerous startups reportedly struggle because they neglect this essential organizational and operational scaffolding, indicating sustained viability depends heavily on sound business foundations rather than advanced technology alone.
Industry observers should note that future investment criteria will increasingly emphasize robust operational frameworks alongside technological prowess for market longevity.
From TheSequence, "The Sequence Opinion #884: Self-Driving Labs: The Laboratory That Chooses Its Next Experiment."
This report details the emergence of self-driving laboratories, which represent a significant advancement in automated scientific research capability.
These systems integrate artificial intelligence with automated hardware to conduct iterative experimentation cycles that exceed simple scripted automation through active learning.
They operate by continuously cycling through design, making, testing, and then crucially, using resulting knowledge to formulate subsequent, more informed experimental designs.

This methodology allows the laboratory process to intelligently traverse complex problem spaces while systematically eliminating unsuccessful areas of investigation, effectively enabling machines to decide their own next investigative step based on prior data.
The development signals a marked shift toward adaptive research paradigms within advanced technology sectors.
From Robotics & Automation News, "The Small Component Problem Behind More Reliable Industrial Robots."
The report details that achieving highly precise and fast coordination in industrial robotics necessitates addressing power stability at the component level to ensure overall system reliability.

Sophisticated operations involving vision systems and microcontrollers are susceptible to disruption from external electrical surges or noise generated by adjacent heavy machinery on shared power grids, thereby compromising sub-millimeter movement accuracy.
Consequently, stabilizing the power supply for individual components is identified as a critical pathway toward more dependable industrial automation solutions.
The industry focus will therefore shift toward integrating advanced power conditioning directly into core robotic subsystems to maintain operational integrity.
From The Robot Report, "ARM Institute expands RoboticsCareer.org into physical AI."
This update significantly broadens the job platform's scope to encompass professional opportunities within the specialized field of physical Artificial Intelligence.
The revised RoboticsCareer.org now features an expanded catalog detailing emerging roles in this domain.
It functions as a direct conduit, connecting corporations with personnel possessing requisite expertise.
The expansion methodology directly addresses the observed increase in industry need for skilled professionals specializing in embodied AI systems.
This development signals a deepening integration of advanced AI into tangible machinery and real-world operational environments across various sectors.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Why Automating the Diagnostics Lab Won’t Rescue a Weak Validation Process."
This report examines the scope of laboratory automation and concludes that technological advancement alone cannot rectify underlying flaws in validation procedures.

The article details how existing robotic sample handling, automated liquid handlers, and advanced specimen tracking systems demonstrably increase throughput and reduce manual errors within diagnostic facilities.
As test volumes continue to rise, these proven technologies alleviate strain on human personnel, solidifying integration as a current operational standard rather than a future possibility.
Consequently, the industry must recognize that enhancing lab workflow requires addressing procedural deficiencies directly, irrespective of the degree of automation implemented.
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: A tutorial to make your quadruped robot on ROS."
The report details a guide demonstrating the construction of a quadruped robot utilizing ROS2 Jazzy.
The necessary codebase for this project is available via a dedicated GitHub repository provided by the author.
This specific robot implementation was built using an esp32 microcontroller paired with a Raspberry Pi, providing a detailed blueprint for replication.
Readers are encouraged to submit any feedback regarding the tutorial or the physical build process to the source material.
Such accessible hardware guides accelerate practical application development within the field of embodied AI and robotics research platforms.
From The Robot Report, "Robust.AI chooses Aptiv PULSE sensor for Gen 3 Carter mobile robot."
The report details Robust.AI's integration of the Aptiv PULSE sensor into its next-generation Carter mobile robot platform.
This implementation facilitates advanced sensor fusion capabilities within the unit.
The system achieves this by combining disparate data streams originating from radar and visual sensors using dedicated artificial intelligence processing power.
Factually, this technological combination empowers the Carter robot to navigate environments with increased safety margins, particularly in areas characterized by human presence.
Industry analysts note that this incorporation marks a significant step forward in real-time robotic interaction capabilities for autonomous mobile systems.
From The Robot Report, "Hirebotics offers no-code, explosion-proof cobot for painting."
This report details a new robotic system designed specifically for automated, safe painting processes in industrial settings.
Hirebotics introduced the Cobraxt Painter, which functions as a collaborative robot or cobot featuring no-code programming capabilities and explosion-proof design specifications.
The operational methodology requires that components be placed within a designated paint booth, where the robots then autonomously execute all required spraying operations on those stationary parts.
This development marks an advancement toward safer and more accessible robotic integration in specialized manufacturing tasks.
Further industry adoption is anticipated as these turnkey solutions become available to wider sectors requiring precision coating applications.
And that's your robotics roundup for June 26th!
From Sony’s speedy sweep with ROS 2 to all the cool tech in between.
Thanks for tuning in; keep exploring what amazing machines can do!
Bye for now.
